metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

records.display_name: Wood blewit group / Clitocybe nuda group
records.culinary_uses: Good edible when properly identified and cooked; flavor is earthy/aromatic. Avoid old soggy specimens and confirm spore print/ID against purple Cortinarius look-alikes.
records.general_notes: A practical gilled entry for the guide because the color, smell, and late-season habit are all useful identification cues.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly fall after cool weather begins.
cap_shape: Convex with inrolled margin when young
Wavy or uplifted margin in age
habitat: Leaf litter and organic debris in woods

records.scientific_name: Clitocybe nuda group / wood blewit complex
records.field_identification: Young mushrooms are dull purple, lilac, or lavender on the cap, gills, and stem, then fade toward buff or brown. Gills are attached, the sweet/fragrant odor can be noticeable, there is no veil, and the spore print is pinkish rather than rusty brown.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good cooked edible, but confirm it is a blewit and not a purple Cortinarius.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Clitocybe nuda
use_links.notes: Blewit color changes, attached gills, pinkish spore print, odor, habitat, and Cortinarius comparison.

records.look_alike_notes: Purple Cortinarius species can look similar but have rusty-brown spore prints and often cobwebby cortina remnants. Wood blewits have a pinkish print, no cortina, and commonly grow in leaf litter or other organic debris. Color alone is not enough.
records.edibility_detail: Edible only after confident identification and cooking. Confirm the pinkish-buff spore print and absence of a Cortinarius-style rusty brown spore print/cortina; avoid raw use, old soggy specimens, and any purple-brown mushroom with rusty spores or unpleasant earthy/chemical odor.
records.quality_notes: Good earthy/aromatic edible when fresh and properly identified; older specimens fade brown and become harder to identify.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook thoroughly. Use only fresh specimens that match blewit traits and have a pale pinkish-buff spore print.
records.risk_reason: Purple Cortinarius species can look similar but have rusty-brown spores and may include dangerous kidney-toxin species.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with toxic Cortinarius: delayed kidney injury risk. General misidentification can cause serious poisoning depending on species.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Purple/lilac cap, gills, and stem fade with age; older specimens can look tan/brown. Blewits have pale pinkish-buff spores and no cortina; Cortinarius has rusty-brown spores and often cobwebby veil remnants.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat a suspected blewit unless the spore print is pale pinkish, there is no cortina or rusty spore dust, and the mushroom matches fresh blewit traits.
records.caution_reason: Cortinarius look-alike risk; spore print is required.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — dangerous Cortinarius confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Blewits are not beginner-safe because purple Cortinarius/webcaps can overlap visually and some Cortinarius can damage kidneys. Purple color alone is not enough.
required_checks: No rusty spore dust or rusty veil remnants
Fresh lilac/purple traits may fade with age
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Rusty-brown spores or cortina remnants mean do not eat as blewit.

lookalike_traits: Cobwebby cortina or rusty veil remnants
Rusty/brown dust on gills or stem
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Rusty-brown spores or cortina remnants mean do not eat as blewit.

edible_use.notes: Cook thoroughly. Use only fresh specimens that match blewit traits and have a pale pinkish-buff spore print; reject Cortinarius-like rusty spores or cortina remnants.

records.notes: Shared warning-group record for red/orange-pored boletes and red/yellow boletes that bruise rapidly or strongly blue. This is a caution anchor, not a food target.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Treat red/orange pores plus rapid or strong blue staining as a stop sign unless a qualified expert confirms the identification.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food. Red/orange-pored boletes and red/yellow boletes that bruise rapidly or strongly blue are difficult to separate; some can cause significant gastrointestinal illness, and beginner separation is unreliable.
records.general_notes: This is a safety grouping, not a single species. It catches the red/orange-pored and strong blue-staining bolete lane so users do not accidentally treat all boletes as edible.

stem_feature: Often reticulate or dotted; variable by species
smell: Variable; avoid if unpleasant or uncertain
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Olive-brown to brown in many boletes
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly summer into fall; treat as caution whenever found.

edible_use.notes: Avoid for food. Several red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes can cause significant gastrointestinal illness, and beginner separation is unreliable.

records.scientific_name: Red/orange-pored and strong blue-staining bolete warning group; includes bicolor-like, sensibilis/pseudosensibilis-type, red-mouth/lurid, and similar caution boletes as field context.
records.field_identification: Umbrella caution group for boletes with red to orange pore surfaces and/or rapid, strong blue staining. Check pore color, bruising speed, flesh color change, stem reticulation or dots, cap color, smell, and tree association. In this guide, red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes are not food targets.
records.mushroom_card_note: Red/orange pores or strong blue staining = caution lane. Do not eat from this group unless expert-confirmed.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Key to red-capped, blue-staining boletes
use_links.notes: Explains why red-capped / blue-staining bolete IDs are difficult and often require advanced work.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus bicolor
use_links.notes: Useful comparison for true bicolor traits versus stronger blue-staining/red-pored confusion.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus page six
use_links.notes: Keys eastern blue-staining boletes and related confusing taxa.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus sensibilis
use_links.notes: Representative red/yellow pore, blue-staining caution bolete.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus pseudosensibilis
use_links.notes: Representative red/yellow pore, blue-staining caution comparison.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Frost's bolete
use_links.notes: Representative red-pored bolete used for caution-group comparison.

records.quality_notes: Representative caution group, not one species. Images and notes show mixed examples of red-pored and/or blue-staining risk patterns.
records.eating_use_notes: No food use recommended in this guide.
records.risk_reason: Many red-capped, red-pored, and blue-staining boletes are hard to separate; some are poisonous or not recommended, and names/concepts have shifted.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, weakness, and dehydration.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Red/orange pore surfaces, rapid blue staining, mixed red/yellow bolete color patterns, and confusing online/field-guide naming.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat red/orange-pored or fast/strong blue-staining boletes unless keyed by an expert.
records.caution_reason: Shared caution group; red/orange pores and fast strong blue staining are stop signs for this app.

records.notes: Large ruffled mass with noodle-like folds rather than gills, pores, or teeth.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Use young, clean specimens. Slice or tear into ribbons after careful cleaning; good sautéed, in soups, or dried if clean enough.
records.general_notes: Eastern North American cauliflower mushrooms are often treated around the Sparassis spathulata/americana complex. Use as a field-group entry rather than overpromising one species name.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young, clean, and confidently identified. Dirt, bugs, and sand collect deep in the folds, so discard old, waterlogged, or hard-to-clean specimens.

underside: Wrinkled folds; no gills or pores
substrate: Near conifers or hardwoods depending species

records.field_identification: Large cream to pale yellow mass of flattened, ruffled, noodle-like folds at the base of trees or from buried roots. It looks like a loose cauliflower or egg noodles, not an upright coral with many tiny branch tips.
records.mushroom_card_note: Ruffled noodle-like mass at tree bases; good when young and clean, but it can be a dirt-and-bug hotel.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Sparassis spathulata
use_links.notes: Field identification and comparison source.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Sparassis americana
use_links.notes: North American cauliflower mushroom comparison.

edible_use.notes: Good edible when young, clean, and confidently identified. Dirt, bugs, and sand collect deep in the folds, so discard old, waterlogged, or hard-to-clean specimens.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — coral/rosette confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Cauliflower mushroom needs noodle/ribbon-like folds, soft pliable flesh, and root/wood association, not upright brittle coral branches.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Brittle upright coral branches are not cauliflower mushroom.

dangerous_lookalikes.display_name: Pale coral fungi / false coral-type mushrooms
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Brittle upright coral branches are not cauliflower mushroom.

lookalike_traits: Pored fan fronds in gray-brown rosette
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Pores on fronds route to hen/polypore, not Sparassis.

lookalike_traits: Separate little caps on branching stalks

source_review_notes: Route remains edible-with-caution/prep-required. Current MushroomExpert links support ID/taxonomy/comparison; add explicit edible-use/prep/cleaning support in a future source-hygiene pass.

records.notes: Irregular black charcoal-like mass on living birch with warm brown interior.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal and modern research interest, mostly as extracts/tea; evidence and safety vary, and it should not be treated as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Not a table mushroom. Traditionally simmered or steeped as a medicinal/traditional tea or decoction; treat as medicinal use, not a normal food mushroom.
records.non_edible_severity: Not a table mushroom / medicinal tea caution
records.edibility_detail: Medicinal/traditional tea use only; not a normal edible/table mushroom.
records.general_notes: Not a culinary mushroom; file under medicinal/traditional tea and other uses rather than normal edible mushrooms.
medicinalTerms: Kidney injury risk with heavy use

growth_form: Sterile conk / black mass on birch
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round on living birch trunks.

records.other_uses: Excellent practical tinder / ember carrier. The dry rusty-brown inner material catches a spark readily, smolders like a coal, and can help start or carry fire; scrape, crumble, or powder the inner material. If damp or freshly harvested, expose or dry the inner material first for best spark-catching.

medicinal.summary: Traditional Chaga tea/decoction/supplement use; not a food recommendation or treatment claim.
medical_terms: Kidney injury risk with heavy use
medicinal.preparation_notes: Usually simmered or steeped as tea/decoction; stronger extracts/supplements are a different risk category than occasional beverage-style use.
medicinal.warnings: Use moderation. Chaga can be high in oxalates and case reports link heavy or long-term powder use with kidney injury. It may also increase bleeding risk with anticoagulant/antiplatelet drugs and may affect blood sugar, so avoid casual supplement-style use with kidney disease, bleeding disorders, diabetes drugs, blood thinners, pregnancy, or before surgery without medical guidance.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Inonotus obliquus
use_links.notes: Black sterile conk on birch and sterile/fertile body distinction.

use_links.label: Review article: Inonotus obliquus research background
use_links.notes: Research/background link only; not a treatment recommendation.

use_links.label: Memorial Sloan Kettering: Chaga Mushroom
use_links.notes: Frames chaga as folk medicine/supplement material and notes safety concerns including bleeding, blood-sugar, and kidney/oxalate risks.

records.field_identification: Black, cracked, charcoal-like sterile conk protruding from living birch; interior is orange-brown, not wood.
records.look_alike_notes: Birch polypore and hoof fungus form recognizable brackets; chaga is a black, cracked sterile mass with orange-brown interior, usually on living birch.
records.edibility_notes: Medicinal/traditional tea use only; not a normal edible/table mushroom.

edible_use.notes: Traditionally prepared as tea/decoction; treated here as medicinal/traditional use rather than normal food.

records.mushroom_card_note: Birch conk used for medicinal/traditional tea/decoction; not a normal edible mushroom. Use caution with chronic/heavy use and medication or kidney concerns.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Recognition and comparison only. Do not consume from this entry.
records.general_notes: Useful as a group entry because amateurs often meet the field concept before they sort out the exact Russula name. Flexible gills are a meaningful criterion here. Representative Commons images use Russula cyanoxantha to illustrate the mild flexible-gill charcoal-burner group.
records.food_quality: Source review / not recommended from this app entry

research_notes: MushroomExpert supports the Russula cyanoxantha / flexible-gill field concept for ID but does not clear edible use.
Broad Russula food routing needs stronger edible-use support and caution copy before review can be cleared.

records.image_coverage_note: Exact public-usable image not currently verified; visible images removed pending exact file-page rights metadata.
records.image_rights_notes: Visible Commons image(s) removed in v4.3.176 because stored metadata did not include exact file-page author/license/license-url rights details; do not restore until exact rights are verified.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Russula cyanoxantha
use_links.notes: Supports the Russula cyanoxantha / flexible-gill field concept, including flexible/greasy gills, mild taste, white spore print, and North American species-complex caution; this is ID support only, not edible-use clearance.

records.edibility_detail: Recognition-only HOLD. Exact species-group treatment and safety clearance remain unresolved.
records.mushroom_card_note: Flexible-gilled white-spored Russula concept; exact species and edible use remain under review.
records.safety: Recognition-only / source review needed; not a food target from this entry
records.risk_reason: Broad Russula-group food framing is not source-cleared here. Mild taste is not enough by itself; confirm no ring, no volva/cup, no latex, white-to-cream spore print, and reject acrid/hot/burning Russulas.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, and source-review context rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
source_review_notes: v4.3.179 normalized this broad Russula group to ID/caution because current active sources support identification only, not edible-use clearance.
Future food routing would require a strong edible-use source for the Russula cyanoxantha / mild flexible-gill concept plus caution framing for Russula ID difficulty.
MushroomExpert supports the flexible/greasy young gills, mottled purple-green cap, white print, mild taste, and North American species-complex warning.
Broad Russula food routing remains rejected until a strong edible-use source and safer species/group framing are available; exact image rights are also still missing.
records.scientific_name: Russula cyanoxantha group / flexible-gilled Russula complex
records.overview: Recognition record for the Russula cyanoxantha flexible-gill field concept: mottled purple, green, olive, or grayish caps; white print; mild taste; and unusually flexible, greasy-feeling young gills. North American material may represent more than one species.
records.field_identification: Confirm a brittle white stem and flesh, no ring or volva, white attached gills that are flexible and greasy-feeling when young, a white spore print, mild taste only after expert confirmation, and a purple-green-olive mottled cap that may show fine radial veins.

records.notes: Bright orange and yellow shelving bracket with pores below, not true gills.
records.culinary_uses: Young, tender shelves are a good edible mushroom after thorough cooking. Trim away tough bases and avoid old shelves; some people get stomach upset even from correctly identified chicken of the woods.
records.general_notes: Some people react to it, especially older specimens or those from some conifers.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young and tender, but not universally tolerated; avoid old, tough, bug-ridden, or conifer-grown material unless you know the local Laetiporus species well.

mushroom_profile.season_note: May through November in eastern hardwood records, but most typical in summer and fall; early spring finds are less common.

records.field_identification: Bright orange to yellow overlapping shelves growing from wood, with a pore surface underneath rather than gills. The texture is soft and fleshy when young, becoming tough with age; host tree and pore color help separate Laetiporus species.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good edible when young and cooked, but tolerance varies; confirm pores, wood growth, and host tree.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Laetiporus sulphureus
use_links.notes: Sulphur shelf field traits, pore surface, host/substrate notes, and edibility caution.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Laetiporus overview
use_links.notes: Separates common Laetiporus species and host patterns.

use_links.label: Tom Volk / UW-La Crosse: Laetiporus cincinnatus and chicken-of-the-woods
use_links.notes: Supports edible use when correctly identified and well cooked; raw-use stomach-upset risk; young tender/growing-edge quality; and Laetiporus species/host distinctions.

records.quality_notes: Young tender margins are best; older shelves become chalky, woody, sour, or bug-riddled.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook thoroughly. Use young tender portions. Try a small first serving.
records.risk_reason: Great Lakes Laetiporus species are not all the same. Conifer-associated forms are more often linked with bad reactions than classic hardwood chicken of the woods.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract and possible neurologic/systemic symptoms in sensitive people; vomiting, chills, and unusual reactions have been reported.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Bright orange/yellow shelf mushroom on living or dead trees; host tree matters. Conifer or hemlock growth in the Great Lakes should be higher caution than classic hardwood growth.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat old, tough, buggy, or conifer-growing chicken of the woods. For confirmed young hardwood specimens, eat a small amount first.
records.caution_reason: Substrate/species and individual-tolerance risk; high food value does not make every Laetiporus safe for every person.

lookalike_risk.label: High — orange wood mushroom and substrate/species caution
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — orange wood mushroom and substrate/species caution
lookalike_risk.summary: Chicken of the woods has pores, not gills. Risk includes poisonous orange gilled clusters and old/conifer/atypical Laetiporus tolerance issues.
required_checks: Reject gills, old/tough/sour/bug-ridden material, and uncertain conifer-associated specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: If it has gills, it is not chicken of the woods.

target_traits: Bright orange/yellow shelves with pore surface underneath
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it has gills, it is not chicken of the woods.

target_traits: Young tender hardwood shelves when target fits
lookalike_traits: Old, chalky, sour, bug-ridden, conifer-associated, or atypical material
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Old, tough, sour, bug-ridden, or uncertain-substrate Laetiporus stays out of the pan.

source_review_notes: Route remains edible-with-caution/prep-required. MushroomExpert links support ID/comparison and Laetiporus species context.

Tom Volk / UW-La Crosse supports chicken-of-the-woods as edible when the right young/tender portions are used and properly cooked; it also supports raw-use stomach-upset risk, species/host distinctions, and older or non-target portions being poor eating.

records.notes: White coral-like tooth fungus with many branches carrying hanging spines.
records.medicinal_uses: Hericium species have medicinal/supplement interest, but this entry is treated primarily as food.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible when young and white. Cook gently; older yellowing specimens lose quality.
records.general_notes: Another Hericium ally that benefits from side-by-side comparison with lion's mane and bear's head tooth.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible Hericium when young, white, and tender after confident identification. Confirm short teeth arranged along delicate branching structures on dead hardwood; avoid yellowing, brittle, dirty, or decaying specimens and separate it from bear’s head tooth and lion’s mane by branching and tooth placement.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer through fall on hardwood logs.

records.field_identification: Branched white Hericium with short teeth hanging in rows along the branches, usually on dead hardwood.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice toothed edible; short teeth hang along delicate branches on hardwood.

records.look_alike_notes: Coral tooth has many branches with teeth along the branches; bear's head tooth has longer teeth clustered near branch tips, while lion's mane is a denser unbranched clump.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — edible Hericium confusion / condition risk
lookalike_risk.short_label: Moderate — edible Hericium confusion / condition risk
lookalike_risk.summary: Hericium risk is mostly edible species confusion and condition: verify white toothed growth on hardwood and reject yellowed/soggy/old material.
required_checks: Check branching/tooth arrangement if species-level matters
Reject yellowed, brown, soggy, or old specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: No teeth, no Hericium; use Hericium group-level if species separation is uncertain.

target_traits: Wood-growing Hericium mass with hanging teeth
lookalike_traits: Terrestrial cap-and-stem mushroom with teeth under a cap
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Cap-and-stem teeth on soil = Hydnum-style hedgehog, not Hericium.

lookalike_traits: Upright coral branches without hanging teeth

records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Dig up the entire base of any white-gilled mushroom; if there is a cup, sack, bulb, volva, or uncertainty, stop.
records.edibility_detail: Deadly poisonous. Not edible under any circumstance.
records.general_notes: One of the highest-priority caution mushrooms in the guide. The combination of white gills, ring, and volva matters more than almost anything else in the gilled lane.

substrate: on ground; mycorrhizal, not growing from wood
ring: present; high skirtlike annulus, but fragile specimens can lose visible ring evidence
mushroom_profile.volva: present; white sacklike cup around base, sometimes buried
staining: usually none; may age slightly yellowish or pinkish in some specimens
smell: not distinctive when young; old specimens may smell foul/sickly sweet
habitat: On ground in oak and hardwood woods

records.scientific_name: Amanita bisporigera and closely related eastern North American white destroying angels; do not use European-only names such as Amanita virosa or Amanita verna as the main regional label.
records.field_identification: All-white mushroom with white cap, white free or nearly free gills, white stem, and white flesh. Persistent high skirtlike ring on the upper stem may be present. White sacklike volva/cup at the swollen base; the base may be underground, so the whole mushroom must be dug up and checked for identification. The image set includes a base/volva example because this is a life-or-death field mark. Cap is smooth/bald, dry to slightly sticky, oval when young and convex to flat with age. Often associated with oaks and other hardwoods; fruits in summer and fall. Old specimens may develop an unpleasant sickly-sweet or rotting odor.
records.mushroom_card_note: Do not eat any white gilled mushroom with a ring or any hint of a cup/volva at the base. If the base was not dug up and checked, treat it as unsafe.

records.poisoning_effects: Delayed vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps, dehydration, apparent recovery, liver injury, kidney failure, coma, and possible death.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Destroying Angel Group. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.look_alike_notes: Meadow Mushroom Agaricus Group: Agaricus has pink to chocolate-brown mature gills and a dark brown spore print, not white gills and a white spore print; young buttons can still fool people. Puffballs: Young Amanita buttons can resemble puffballs before the cap opens. Slice any puffball-like mushroom top-to-bottom; any outline of cap, gills, stem, ring, or volva means reject. Volvopluteus Stubble Rosegill: Volvopluteus has pink mature gills and pink spore print and lacks a ring; destroying angels keep white gills/spores and have ring plus volva.

records.toxicity_notes: Contains amatoxins with delayed GI illness, apparent recovery, then possible liver and kidney failure.
records.caution_reason: Amatoxin risk; white-gilled Amanitas can kill and the buried volva/base is often missed.
records.risk_reason: Contains amatoxins. A person may feel fine for hours, then develop severe gastrointestinal symptoms, seem to improve, and later develop liver and kidney failure.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Liver and kidneys; delayed vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps, dehydration, apparent recovery, liver injury, kidney failure, coma, and possible death.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: White gills, white spore print, possible ring, and a cup-like volva at the base that may be buried in soil or leaf litter. Young button-stage Amanitas can be mistaken for puffballs or edible white mushrooms if not cut open and dug up completely.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat any white-gilled mushroom with a volva, cup, sack, bulb, or unknown buried base. Dig up the entire base; if Amanita traits are present or uncertain, do not eat.
records.danger_notes: Delayed symptoms are more dangerous than quick stomach upset. If vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain begins 6 or more hours after eating wild mushrooms, treat it as a medical emergency and contact Poison Control or emergency care.

field_measurements.stem: 5.5–14 cm long; 0.5–2 cm thick / about 2.2–5.5 in long; 0.2–0.8 in thick
field_measurements.notes: Size source: MushroomExpert for Amanita bisporigera, used as the eastern destroying-angel anchor. Check the enlarged base/volva; the base may be underground.

records.notes: Large spring shelf polypore on hardwoods with tan cap, brown flattened scales, angular pores running down the stem, and a mealy/cucumber-like odor. Use only tender young edges.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when very young and tender; older caps become leathery. Good sliced thin, sautéed, or dried for stock when still fragrant.
records.edibility_detail: Edible young only; quality drops quickly with age.
records.general_notes: Mostly worth collecting young; older specimens get leathery fast.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Most useful as a young spring mushroom before it toughens.

records.field_identification: Large scaly tan-to-brown shelf with big angular pores and a watermelon-rind/cucumber-like odor.
records.mushroom_card_note: Spring polypore; edible only when young and tender. Older shelves are tough.

use_links.label: NPS: Dryad's saddle overview

records.look_alike_notes: Older dryad's saddle becomes leathery; birch polypore and artist's conk are tougher shelf fungi without the big scaly cap and cucumber/watermelon odor.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — quality and bracket-polypore confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Dryad’s saddle is mainly an age/texture and bracket-polypore confusion issue, not a deadly look-alike problem.
required_checks: Check scaly cap and cucumber/watermelon-rind odor
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Gills, teeth, or old leathery shelves mean not eating-quality dryad’s saddle.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Teeth under a cap = not dryad’s saddle.

lookalike_traits: Large, leathery, tough, bug-ridden, woody flesh
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Old tough shelves are not eating-quality material.

source_review_notes: Current source mix is acceptable for the young-only/fair-quality food concept; v4.3.179 only clarified the NPS source note.

records.display_name: False morel / Gyromitra group — see False Morels / Gyromitra Group
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Any brain-like, wrinkled, folded, saddle-shaped, or beefsteak-looking spring mushroom is a hard no unless it is confirmed as true Morchella by lengthwise cut and cap attachment.
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous; some cases can be severe or fatal. Not a safe substitute for true morels.

substrate: on ground in woods, forest edges, burns, sandy soils, or disturbed spring habitats depending species
mushroom_profile.season_note: spring, early summer in colder northern sites
underside: no gills; spore-bearing surface on folded/lobed cap
underside_color: similar to cap or paler, species-dependent
mushroom_profile.spore_print: not applicable/useful in the usual cap-and-paper sense; microscopy may be needed for species-level Gyromitra identification

records.scientific_name: Gyromitra species likely to be encountered around Michigan morel season, especially Gyromitra esculenta ('beefsteak morel') and related false morel groups. This entry is for Gyromitra, not all Verpa species, though Verpa should remain linked as non-edible morel look-alikes.
records.field_identification: Wrinkled, folded, lobed, saddle-like, or brain-like cap rather than a regular pitted honeycomb cap. Often reddish brown, chestnut, mahogany, tan, or darker brown; color varies by species and age. Interior is not cleanly hollow like true morels; stem may be solid, chambered, cottony, or irregular inside. Cap attachment differs from true morels and can be irregular rather than neatly attached along the lower edge. Often appears in spring during or near morel season, sometimes close to true morel habitat. No gills; this is an ascomycete false-morel form, but it belongs in searchable mushroom danger records.
records.mushroom_card_note: If it is not hollow from cap tip through stem base and does not have true morel pits, do not eat it. Any brain-like, wrinkled, folded, or beefsteak-looking spring mushroom is a hard no.

records.overview: Searchable danger entry for False Morels / Gyromitra Group. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.look_alike_notes: Morels: True morels have a pitted cap and are hollow inside from top to bottom when cut lengthwise. Gyromitra is folded/brain-like and may be solid, chambered, or cottony inside. Half Free Morel: Half-free morels have a true morel pit pattern and a partially attached cap; false morels can have irregular folds and unsafe internal structure. Verpa Group: Verpa species are also non-edible look-alikes in the app; cap attachment and interior structure should be used to keep both Verpa and Gyromitra out of edible morel decisions.
records.poisoning_effects: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dizziness, headache, confusion, delirium, seizures, coma, hepatitis, jaundice, clotting problems, and possible death.
records.toxicity_notes: Gyromitrin/monomethylhydrazine-type toxin risk; can affect GI tract, liver, kidneys, nervous system, and blood.

use_links.label: Michigan DNR: Morel Mushroom Identification Tips
use_links.notes: Michigan relevance, Gyromitra warning, true morel vs false morel traits, do-not-eat guidance

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Gyromitra — The False Morels
use_links.notes: group scope, macroscopic and microscopic identification notes

use_links.label: NCBI Bookshelf / StatPearls: Gyromitra Mushroom Toxicity
use_links.notes: gyromitrin, monomethylhydrazine, affected systems, clinical symptoms

records.risk_reason: Gyromitra species may contain gyromitrin, which converts to monomethylhydrazine-type toxins. Toxicity is variable; one person may get little effect while another becomes seriously ill.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract, liver, kidneys, nervous system, and blood; nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dizziness, headache, confusion, delirium, seizures, coma, hepatitis, jaundice, and clotting problems can occur.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Wrinkled, lobed, brain-like, saddle-shaped, or irregular caps; cap may not be fully attached like a true morel; interior may be chambered rather than cleanly hollow.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat false morels, beefsteak morels, Verpa, or any morel-like mushroom unless it is confirmed as a true Morchella and cut lengthwise to verify a hollow interior and proper cap attachment.
records.caution_reason: Gyromitrin risk and true-morel confusion; “looks kind of like a morel” is not enough.
records.danger_notes: Delayed symptoms are more dangerous than quick stomach upset. If vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain begins 6 or more hours after eating wild mushrooms, treat it as a medical emergency and contact Poison Control or emergency care.
records.redirect_note: False morel / Gyromitra group is covered under the canonical false-morel record.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Good cooked food when confidently identified as an edible Agaricus. Cook young, clean specimens; discard old, buggy, phenolic-smelling, or yellow-staining specimens.
records.edibility_detail: Treat as caution / not for meals in this guide until species-level edibility is clarified.
records.general_notes: One of the everyday 'looks like a store mushroom' entries, but still a caution species because white Amanitas and yellowing toxic Agaricus relatives complicate things.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer through fall in open grassy ground.

records.duplicate_note: Field mushroom is a legacy alias for the visible Meadow / field Agaricus complex record.

records.field_identification: White to pale cap in grass, free gills that shift from pink to chocolate brown, dark brown spore print, ring present, no volva, and no strong yellow staining or chemical/phenolic odor.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Agaricus campestris
use_links.notes: Field mushroom traits, Agaricus caution, and poisonous look-alikes.

records.look_alike_notes: Do not use vague white lawn mushrooms. Deadly Amanitas have white gills/spores and a volva; yellow-staining Agaricus types stain bright yellow and often smell chemical/phenolic.
records.quality_notes: Store-mushroom-like food value when the species is good; older specimens get dark, buggy, or unpleasant.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook thoroughly. Do not use any white lawn mushroom unless gill color, spore print, base, ring, bruising, odor, and habitat all fit the edible Agaricus target.
records.risk_reason: Yellow-staining/phenolic Agaricus can cause GI poisoning, and deadly Amanitas can be confused with white lawn mushrooms if gills, spores, base, ring, odor, and staining are not checked.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Yellow-staining Agaricus: gastrointestinal tract with nausea, cramps, diarrhea, and vomiting. Amanita confusion: liver and kidney failure risk.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Free gills that start pink and become chocolate-brown, brown spore print, ring, no volva, staining reaction, and odor all matter. Bright yellow staining at the stem base or phenolic/ink/iodine smell is a stop sign.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat Agaricus with bright yellow staining at the stem base or a chemical/phenolic odor. Do not eat white-gilled mushrooms unless Amanita has been ruled out by digging up the entire base.
records.caution_reason: Yellow-staining/phenolic Agaricus can cause GI poisoning, and deadly Amanitas can be confused with white lawn mushrooms if gills, spores, base, ring, odor, and staining are not checked.

edible_use.notes: Cook only after careful Agaricus identification. Confirm pink-to-chocolate gills, brown spore print, no volva, no bright yellow base staining, and no phenolic/ink-like odor.

records.notes: Only edible when the interior is pure white and uniform all the way through.
records.culinary_uses: Good edible only while the interior is pure white and uniform. Slice open every specimen; discard any yellowing, browning, developing gills, or odd structure.
records.general_notes: A cut-open check is mandatory because immature deadly amanitas can fool people when still buttoned.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young and solid white inside.

mushroom_profile.spore_print: No conventional cap-on-paper print; the internal gleba changes from white to olive-yellow and eventually brownish spore dust.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer into fall in open ground and edges.
flesh_color: Pure white and uniform when immature
Olive-yellow to brownish as spores mature

records.field_identification: Large white ball-like puffball with no developing cap, stem, gills, or internal outline when cut open. The interior must be solid, featureless, and pure white; yellow, olive, brown, purple, black, marbled, gelatinous, or structured interiors are reject signs.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good edible only when cut-open flesh is pure white all the way through.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Calvatia gigantea
use_links.notes: Giant puffball traits and warnings about immature gilled mushrooms/earthballs.

records.look_alike_notes: Slice every puffball vertically. Giant puffballs must be solid white and uniform inside; any developing cap/gills/stem structure points toward an immature gilled mushroom, including deadly Amanita risk. Cut every puffball from top to bottom; eat only specimens with a solid, featureless, pure-white interior, and discard anything showing gills, a stem outline, gelatin, yellowing, browning, or dark flesh. Earthballs and Amanita buttons are the primary danger checks.
records.caution_reason: Interior condition is the safety gate; Amanita buttons and earthballs are the major mistakes.
records.quality_notes: Mild blank-canvas flavor; can be bland or sponge-like unless browned well.
records.eating_use_notes: Slice open every specimen. Eat only when the interior is solid, uniform, marshmallow-white. Brown well.
records.risk_reason: Old puffballs become inedible, and young Amanita buttons or earthballs can be mistaken for puffballs if not cut open.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Old/contaminated puffballs can cause GI upset. Amanita confusion can cause liver/kidney failure. Earthball confusion can cause GI irritation and sometimes neurologic/cardiovascular symptoms.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Round white mushrooms with no visible gills can hide dangerous look-alikes. Edible puffballs must be solid and uniform white inside, with no gills, stem outline, yellow, green, brown, purple, black, or developing cap/stem structure.
records.hard_rule: Cut every puffball from top to bottom. If the inside is not solid pure white with no internal mushroom outline, do not eat it.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — Amanita button / earthball confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: Extreme — Amanita button / earthball confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Puffballs are edible only when the interior is solid, featureless, soft, and pure white.
required_checks: Interior must be solid, soft, featureless, and pure white
Reject thick rind, gelatinous layer, yellow/olive/brown/purple/black/marbled/veined interior, or powdery/darkening flesh
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: If the inside is not solid pure white with no internal mushroom outline, do not eat it.

lookalike_traits: Hidden cap, stem, gills, ring, or cup/volva when cut
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Any internal mushroom outline means do not eat as puffball.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / gastrointestinal and possible systemic effects.
lookalike_traits: Dark, purple, brown, black, marbled, veined, powdery, or tough interior
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Dark, marbled, veined, powdery, or tough interiors mean do not eat.

records.source_note: v4.3.209 separates mature internal gleba color from a conventional deposited spore print.

records.food_quality: Good only when young and pure white inside
records.edibility_detail: Edible only when young, firm, and completely white/uniform inside. Slice every puffball-like mushroom top-to-bottom; discard anything with gills, a cap/stem outline, yellow/olive/brown interior, or marbling.
records.culinary_uses: Cook young, pure-white specimens thoroughly. Do not eat old, yellowing, olive, brown, or powdery specimens.
records.overview: Common woodland puffball with short spines or “gems” on the surface.
records.notes: Useful puffball record, but all puffballs require the slice test to avoid immature Amanita or earthball confusion.
records.field_identification: Small to medium terrestrial puffball with whitish surface covered in short spines/warts that can rub off and leave a net-like pattern. Interior must be solid white when edible.
records.habitat: Woods, trail edges, grassy clearings, and forest litter.

flesh_color: Pure white when edible; yellow/olive/brown when too old
mushroom_profile.spore_print: No conventional cap-on-paper print; mature upper gleba and spore powder are olive-brown and are released through an apical pore by raindrops and wind.
mushroom_profile.hard_rule: Slice every puffball-like mushroom; eat only if the interior is completely white and uniform.

use_links.notes: Gem-studded puffball morphology, internal gleba maturation, and apical-pore spore release.

records.mushroom_card_note: Spiny woodland puffball; slice every specimen and eat only if pure white inside.

records.food_quality: Edible young only when pure white inside
records.edibility_detail: Edible only when young and completely white/uniform inside. Slice every specimen. This species commonly grows on decaying wood, which helps separate it from many terrestrial puffballs.
records.culinary_uses: Cook young pure-white specimens thoroughly. Avoid old/yellow/olive/brown or powdery specimens.
records.overview: Small pear-shaped puffball that often fruits in clusters on decaying wood.
records.notes: Important because the wood-growing habit helps distinguish it from some other puffballs, but the same slice-test rule still applies.
records.field_identification: Pear-shaped puffballs in clusters on rotten logs, stumps, buried wood, or wood-rich soil. Interior must be solid white when edible.
records.habitat: Decaying logs, stumps, buried wood, and wood-rich soil.

growth_form: Clustered enclosed puffballs on dead wood
flesh_color: Pure white when edible; yellow/olive/brown when too old
mushroom_profile.spore_print: No conventional cap-on-paper print; internal gleba changes from white to yellowish or olive and eventually brownish spore dust, released through an apical pore by raindrops and wind.
mushroom_profile.hard_rule: Slice every puffball-like mushroom; eat only if the interior is completely white and uniform.

use_links.notes: Wood-growing clustered puffball morphology, internal gleba maturation, apical-pore release, and taxonomy context.

records.mushroom_card_note: Wood-growing puffball; slice every specimen and eat only if pure white inside.
